November 5, 1996(1996-11-05) (aged 62)  [Los Angeles, California](/wiki/Los_Angeles,_California "Los Angeles, California"), U.S.

[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Eddie+Harris%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Eddie+Harris%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Eddie+Harris%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Eddie+Harris%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(April 2020)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

(October 20, 1934 – November 5, 1996) was an American [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") musician, best known for playing [tenor saxophone](/wiki/Tenor_saxophone "Tenor saxophone") and for introducing the electrically amplified saxophone. He was also fluent on the [electric piano](/wiki/Electric_piano "Electric piano") and organ. His best-known compositions are "Freedom Jazz Dance", popularized by [Miles Davis](/wiki/Miles_Davis "Miles Davis") in 1966, and "Listen Here".[[1]](#cite_note-feather-1)

Musical artist

**Eddie Harris** (October 20, 1934 – November 5, 1996) was an American [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") musician, best known for playing [tenor saxophone](/wiki/Tenor_saxophone "Tenor saxophone") and for introducing the electrically amplified saxophone. He was also fluent on the [electric piano](/wiki/Electric_piano "Electric piano") and organ. His best-known compositions are "Freedom Jazz Dance", popularized by [Miles Davis](/wiki/Miles_Davis "Miles Davis") in 1966, and "Listen Here".[[1]](#cite_note-feather-1)

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eddie_Harris&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

Harris was born and grew up in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago").[[2]](#cite_note-LarkinGE-2) His father was from [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba") and his mother from Mississippi. He studied music under [Walter Dyett](/wiki/Walter_Dyett "Walter Dyett") at [DuSable High School](/wiki/DuSable_High_School "DuSable High School"), as had many other successful Chicago musicians (including [Nat King Cole](/wiki/Nat_King_Cole "Nat King Cole"), [Clifford Jordan](/wiki/Clifford_Jordan "Clifford Jordan"), [Johnny Griffin](/wiki/Johnny_Griffin "Johnny Griffin"), [Gene Ammons](/wiki/Gene_Ammons "Gene Ammons"), [Julian Priester](/wiki/Julian_Priester "Julian Priester"), and others). He later studied music at [Roosevelt University](/wiki/Roosevelt_University "Roosevelt University"); by that time he was proficient on piano, [vibraphone](/wiki/Vibraphone "Vibraphone"), and tenor saxophone. While in college he performed professionally with [Gene Ammons](/wiki/Gene_Ammons "Gene Ammons").

After college, Harris was [drafted](/wiki/Military_draft "Military draft") into the [United States Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army") and while serving in Europe, he was accepted into the 7th Army Band which also included [Don Ellis](/wiki/Don_Ellis "Don Ellis"), [Leo Wright](/wiki/Leo_Wright "Leo Wright"), and [Cedar Walton](/wiki/Cedar_Walton "Cedar Walton").

Leaving military service, Harris worked in New York City before returning to Chicago where he signed a contract with [Vee Jay Records](/wiki/Vee_Jay_Records "Vee Jay Records"). His first album for Vee Jay, *[Exodus to Jazz](/wiki/Exodus_to_Jazz "Exodus to Jazz")*, included his own jazz arrangement of [Ernest Gold](/wiki/Ernest_Gold_(composer) "Ernest Gold (composer)")'s [theme from the movie](/wiki/Theme_of_Exodus "Theme of Exodus") *[Exodus](/wiki/Exodus_(1960_film) "Exodus (1960 film)")*. A shortened version of the track, which featured his playing in the upper register of the tenor saxophone, was heavily played on radio and became the first jazz record ever to be certified gold.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)

The single, "Exodus", climbed into the US [*Billboard* Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100") and reached No. 16 in the U.S. [R&B](/wiki/Hot_R%26B/Hip-Hop_Songs "Hot R&B/Hip-Hop Songs") [chart](/wiki/Record_chart "Record chart").[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) He moved to [Columbia Records](/wiki/Columbia_Records "Columbia Records") in 1964 and then to [Atlantic Records](/wiki/Atlantic_Records "Atlantic Records") the following year where he re-established himself. In 1965, Atlantic released *[The In Sound](/wiki/The_In_Sound_(Eddie_Harris_album) "The In Sound (Eddie Harris album)")*, a [bop](/wiki/Bebop "Bebop") album which won back many of his detractors.

During the next few years, he began to perform on [electric piano](/wiki/Electric_piano "Electric piano") and the electric [Varitone](/wiki/Varitone "Varitone") saxophone,[[1]](#cite_note-feather-1) and to perform a mixture of jazz and [funk](/wiki/Funk "Funk") that sold well in both the jazz and [rhythm and blues](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues") markets. In 1967, his album *[The Electrifying Eddie Harris](/wiki/The_Electrifying_Eddie_Harris "The Electrifying Eddie Harris")* reached second place on the R&B chart. The album's second track, "Listen Here", was issued as a single climbing to No. 11 R&B and No. 45 on the Hot 100. Harris released several different versions of his composition over the years, including both studio and live concert recordings. The first appeared on an early Atlantic album, *Mean Greens*, featuring him on electric piano. He was to re-work the track two years later, stretching it out to over seven minutes in length, for his hit version on which he played saxophone. The entire track appeared on both sides of the Atlantic hit single and edited into two parts. For the 11th Annual Grammy Awards in 1968, Harris was nominated for the Best Instrumental Jazz Performance for Small Group or Soloist with Small Group for the Album – *[The Electrifying Eddie Harris](/wiki/The_Electrifying_Eddie_Harris "The Electrifying Eddie Harris")*.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4)

In 1969, he performed with pianist and vocalist [Les McCann](/wiki/Les_McCann "Les McCann") at the [Montreux Jazz Festival](/wiki/Montreux_Jazz_Festival "Montreux Jazz Festival"). Although the musicians had been unable to rehearse, their session was so successful that a recording of it was released by Atlantic as *[Swiss Movement](/wiki/Swiss_Movement "Swiss Movement")*. This became one of the best-selling jazz albums ever and was nominated during the 13th Annual Grammy Awards (1970) for the Best Jazz Performance – Small Group or Soloist with Small Group.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4)

Harris also came up with the idea of the reed trumpet, playing one for the first time at the 1970 [Newport Jazz Festival](/wiki/Newport_Jazz_Festival "Newport Jazz Festival").[[2]](#cite_note-LarkinGE-2) He moved from Chicago to [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") in the 1970s. From 1970 to 1975, he experimented with new instruments of his own invention (the reed trumpet was a trumpet with a saxophone mouthpiece, the saxobone was a saxophone with a trombone mouthpiece, and the guitorgan was a combination of guitar and organ), with singing the [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues"), with [jazz-rock](/wiki/Jazz-rock "Jazz-rock") (he recorded an album with [Steve Winwood](/wiki/Steve_Winwood "Steve Winwood"), [Jeff Beck](/wiki/Jeff_Beck "Jeff Beck"), [Albert Lee](/wiki/Albert_Lee "Albert Lee"), [Ric Grech](/wiki/Ric_Grech "Ric Grech"), [Zoot Money](/wiki/Zoot_Money "Zoot Money"), [Ian Paice](/wiki/Ian_Paice "Ian Paice") and other rockers).[[2]](#cite_note-LarkinGE-2) He also started singing comic R&B/blues songs, such as "That is Why You're Overweight" and "Eddie Who?".

In 1975, however, he alienated some of his audience with his album *[The Reason Why I'm Talking S--t](/wiki/The_Reason_Why_I%27m_Talking_S--t "The Reason Why I'm Talking S--t")*, which consisted mainly of comedy.[[5]](#cite_note-5) After recording for Atlantic for over 12 years, Harris left the record company after completing his final album for the label in 1977. He then signed with RCA Records and recorded two albums.

Harris died of congestive heart failure in Los Angeles on November 5, 1996, at the age of 62.[[6]](#cite_note-:2-6)

## Discography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eddie_Harris&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Discography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Eddie_Harris.jpg/250px-Eddie_Harris.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eddie_Harris.jpg)

Harris at the [Great American Music Hall](/wiki/Great_American_Music_Hall "Great American Music Hall"), San Francisco, November 22, 1980

### As leader

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eddie_Harris&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: As leader")]

* 1961: *[Exodus to Jazz](/wiki/Exodus_to_Jazz "Exodus to Jazz")* ([Vee-Jay](/wiki/Vee-Jay_Records "Vee-Jay Records"))
* 1961: *[Mighty Like a Rose](/wiki/Mighty_Like_a_Rose_(Eddie_Harris_album) "Mighty Like a Rose (Eddie Harris album)")* (Vee-Jay)
* 1961: *[Jazz for "Breakfast at Tiffany's"](/wiki/Jazz_for_%22Breakfast_at_Tiffany%27s%22 "Jazz for \"Breakfast at Tiffany's\"")* (Vee-Jay)
* 1962: *[A Study in Jazz](/wiki/A_Study_in_Jazz "A Study in Jazz")* (Vee-Jay)

* 1963: *Half and Half* (Vee-Jay)
* 1964: *[For Bird and Bags](/wiki/For_Bird_and_Bags "For Bird and Bags")* ([Exodus](/wiki/Exodus_Records "Exodus Records")) also released as *Sculpture* ([Buddah](/wiki/Buddah_Records "Buddah Records"))

* 1965: *[The In Sound](/wiki/The_In_Sound_(Eddie_Harris_album) "The In Sound (Eddie Harris album)")* ([Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic_Records "Atlantic Records"))
* 1966: *[Mean Greens](/wiki/Mean_Greens "Mean Greens")* (Atlantic)
* 1967: *[The Tender Storm](/wiki/The_Tender_Storm "The Tender Storm")* (Atlantic)
* 1968: *[The Electrifying Eddie Harris](/wiki/The_Electrifying_Eddie_Harris "The Electrifying Eddie Harris")* (Atlantic)
* 1968: *[Plug Me In](/wiki/Plug_Me_In_(album) "Plug Me In (album)")* (Atlantic)

* 1969: *Sculpture* ([Buddah](/wiki/Buddah_Records "Buddah Records")) previously released as *[For Bird and Bags](/wiki/For_Bird_and_Bags "For Bird and Bags")* ([Exodus](/wiki/Exodus_Records "Exodus Records"))
* 1969: *[Free Speech](/wiki/Free_Speech_(album) "Free Speech (album)")* (Atlantic)
* 1970: *[Come on Down!](/wiki/Come_On_Down!_(Eddie_Harris_album) "Come On Down! (Eddie Harris album)")* (Atlantic)

* 1990: *Live at the Moonwalker* (Moonwalker Label)
* 1991: *A Tale of Two Cities* [live] (Night/[Virgin](/wiki/Virgin_Records "Virgin Records")) recorded 1978 and 1983
* 1991: *There Was a Time – Echo of Harlem* ([Enja](/wiki/Enja_Records "Enja Records"))
* 1993: *For You, For Me, For Evermore* ([SteepleChase](/wiki/SteepleChase_Records "SteepleChase Records"))
* 1993: *Yeah You Right* (Lakeside)
* 1993: *Listen Here* (Enja)
* 1993: *Artist's Choice: The Eddie Harris Anthology* ([Rhino](/wiki/Rhino_Records "Rhino Records")) 2-CD
* 1994: *Freedom Jazz Dance* (Musicmasters/BMG)
* 1994: *Vexatious Progressions* (Flying Heart)
* 1994: *The Battle of the Tenors* (Enja) with [Wendell Harrison](/wiki/Wendell_Harrison "Wendell Harrison")
* 1996: *Dancing by a Rainbow* (Enja)[[7]](#cite_note-7)
* 1996: *All The Way Live* ([Milestone](/wiki/Milestone_Records "Milestone Records")) with [Jimmy Smith](/wiki/Jimmy_Smith_(musician) "Jimmy Smith (musician)"); recorded 1981
* 1997: *The Last Concert* (ACT)
* 2005: *Exodus: The Best of the Vee-Jay Years* ([Charly](/wiki/Charly_Records "Charly Records"))
* 2017: *Live: Las Vegas 1985* (Hi-Hat)

### As sideman

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eddie_Harris&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: As sideman")]

**With [Buddy Montgomery](/wiki/Buddy_Montgomery "Buddy Montgomery")**

* *[Ties of Love](/wiki/Ties_of_Love "Ties of Love")* (Landmark, 1987)

**With [Bernard Purdie](/wiki/Bernard_Purdie "Bernard Purdie")**

* *Bernard Purdie's Soul to Jazz* (ACT, 1996)

**With [Cedar Walton](/wiki/Cedar_Walton "Cedar Walton")**

* *[Beyond Mobius](/wiki/Beyond_Mobius "Beyond Mobius")* (RCA, 1976)[[6]](#cite_note-:2-6)

**With [Ellis Marsalis, Jr.](/wiki/Ellis_Marsalis,_Jr. "Ellis Marsalis, Jr.")**

* *Homecoming* (Spindletop, 1985)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

**With [Horace Parlan](/wiki/Horace_Parlan "Horace Parlan")**

* *[Glad I Found You](/wiki/Glad_I_Found_You "Glad I Found You")* (Steeplechase, 1986)

**With [Horace Silver](/wiki/Horace_Silver "Horace Silver")**

* *[Spiritualizing the Senses](/wiki/Spiritualizing_the_Senses "Spiritualizing the Senses")* (Silveto, 1983)
* *[There's No Need to Struggle](/wiki/There%27s_No_Need_to_Struggle "There's No Need to Struggle")* (Silveto, 1983)

**With [John Scofield](/wiki/John_Scofield "John Scofield")**

* *[Hand Jive](/wiki/Hand_Jive_(album) "Hand Jive (album)")* ([Blue Note](/wiki/Blue_Note_Records "Blue Note Records"), 1994)
